description: This figure consists of two subplots for Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel annealed at 700°C to 1200°C for 1 h. Subplot (a) presents engineering stress-engineering strain curves, while subplot (b) shows corresponding true stress-true strain curves plus work hardening rate curves. As annealing temperature increases, both engineering and true stress decrease continuously, while elongation first increases to a peak of 36.6% at 900°C then drops at higher temperatures, leading to the optimal product of strength and ductility of 31 GPa% at 900°C. For specimens annealed below 900°C with κ-carbide precipitates, work hardening rates at true strain <5% are higher than those annealed at ≥900°C without obvious κ-carbide, and the rates drop rapidly after early deformation. Specimens annealed at 900°C and 950°C have similar work hardening rates, while the 950°C specimen fractures earlier. The figure demonstrates that annealing temperature modulates mechanical properties via regulating phase composition and grain size.
